address: 103 King Prajadhipok Road, Phra Singh, Muang Chiang Mai

description.en:
## Overview

Wat Chedi Luang is one of the most historically significant temples in Chiang Mai, dominating the center of the Old City with its massive partially ruined chedi. The name translates to "Temple of the Great Stupa," and even in its damaged state the structure remains an imposing sight that hints at the power and ambition of the Lanna Kingdom at its height. It is also one of the few temples in Chiang Mai that runs a popular Monk Chat program, giving visitors the rare chance to have a conversation with Buddhist monks.

## History

Construction of the chedi began in 1391 under King Saen Muang Ma and was expanded during the 15th century by King Tilokaraj, who raised it to a height of roughly 80 meters, making it the tallest structure in old Chiang Mai. The temple once housed the Emerald Buddha, Thailand's most sacred Buddha image, which was kept here from 1468 until 1551 before being moved to Luang Prabang and eventually to Bangkok's Grand Palace. A devastating earthquake in 1545 toppled the upper third of the chedi, and it was never fully restored. Partial reconstruction was carried out in the 1990s with support from UNESCO and the Japanese government.

## What to See

The ruined chedi is the centerpiece, with naga serpent staircases flanking each side and elephant sculptures lining the base. A replica of the Emerald Buddha sits in a niche on the eastern face. The surrounding grounds include a large viharn housing a standing Buddha image, a towering gum tree believed to protect the city according to local legend, and several smaller shrines. The Monk Chat program operates daily from 9:00 AM to 6:00 PM on the northern side of the grounds, where English-speaking monks welcome questions about Buddhism, Thai culture, and monastic life.

## Practical Info

The temple is open daily from 6:00 AM to 6:00 PM with an entrance fee of 40 THB for foreign adults. Children under 12 enter free. It is centrally located within the Old City and easily reached on foot from most guesthouses in the area. Early morning and late afternoon offer the best light for photography and the thinnest crowds. Dress modestly with shoulders and knees covered, as this is an active monastery.

best_time_to_visit: Early morning or late afternoon for best light and fewer crowds
tips: Join the free Monk Chat program daily from 9 AM to 6 PM on the temple's north side
Look for the city pillar shrine and the ancient gum tree said to protect Chiang Mai
Dress modestly as this is an active monastery with resident monks
